title: The Ballad Of The Rousabout
content:
آ آ آ آ A Rouseabout of rouseabouts, from any land, or none,
آ آ آ آ I bear a nick-name of the bush, and Iï؟½m, a womanï؟½s son;
آ آ آ آ I came from where I campï؟½d last night, and, at the day-dawn glow,
آ آ آ آ I rub the darkness from my eyes, roll up my swag, and go.

آ آ آ آ Some take the track for bitter pride, some for no pride at all,
آ آ آ آ (But, to us all the world is wide when driven to the wall)
آ آ آ آ Some take the track for gain in life, some take the track for loss,
آ آ آ آ And some of us take up the swag as Christ took up the Cross.

آ آ آ آ Some take the track for faith in men, some take the track for doubt,
آ آ آ آ Some flee a squalid home to work their own salvation out.
آ آ آ آ Some dared not see a motherï؟½s tears nor meet a fatherï؟½s face,
آ آ آ آ Born of good Christian families some leap, head-long, from Grace.

آ آ آ آ Oh we are men who fought and rose, or fell from many grades;
آ آ آ آ Some born to lie, and some to pray, weï؟½re men of many trades;
آ آ آ آ Weï؟½re men whose fathers were and are of high and low degree,
آ آ آ آ The sea was open to us and we sailed across the sea.

آ آ آ آ And, were our quarrels wrong or just?, has no place in my song,
آ آ آ آ We seared our souls in puzzling as to what was right or wrong;
آ آ آ آ We judge not and we are not judged, ï؟½tis our philosophy,
آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s something wrong with every ship that sails upon the sea.

آ آ آ آ From shearing shed to shearing shed we tramp to make a cheque,
آ آ آ آ Jack Cornstalk and the neï؟½er-do-weel, the tar-boy and the wreck.
آ آ آ آ We learn the worth of man to man, and this we learn too well,
آ آ آ آ The shanty and the shearing shed are warmer spots in hell!

آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve humped my swag to Bawley Plain, and further out and on;
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve boiled my billy by the Gulf, and boiled it by the Swan,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve thirsted in dry lignum swamps, and thirsted on the sand,
آ آ آ آ And eked the fire with camel dung in Never-Never Land.

آ آ آ آ I know the track from Spencerï؟½s Gulf and north of Cooperï؟½s Creek,
آ آ آ آ Where falls the half-caste to the strong, ï؟½black velvetï؟½ to the weak,
آ آ آ آ (From gold-top Flossie in the Strand to half-caste and the gin,
آ آ آ آ If they had brains, poor animals! weï؟½d teach them how to sin.)

آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve tramped, and camped, and ï؟½shoreï؟½ and drunk with many mates Out Back,
آ آ آ آ And every one to me is Jack because the first was Jack,
آ آ آ آ A ï؟½liferï؟½ sneaked from jail at home, the ï؟½straightestï؟½ mate I met,
آ آ آ آ A ï؟½rattyï؟½ Russian Nihilist, a British Baronet!

آ آ آ آ I know the tucker tracks that feed, or leave one in the lurch,
آ آ آ آ The ï؟½Burgooï؟½ (Presbyterian) track, the ï؟½Murphyï؟½ (Roman Church),
آ آ آ آ But more the man, and not the track, so much as it appears,
آ آ آ آ For ï؟½battlingï؟½ is a trade to learn, and Iï؟½ve served seven years.

آ آ آ آ Weï؟½re haunted by the past at times, and this is very bad,
آ آ آ آ And so we drink till horrors come, lest, sober, we go mad,
آ آ آ آ So much is lost Out Back, so much of hell is realised,
آ آ آ آ A man might skin himself alive and no one be surprised.

آ آ آ آ A rouseabout of rouseabouts, above, beneath regard,
آ آ آ آ I know how soft is this old world, and I have learnt, how hard,
آ آ آ آ A rouseabout of rouseabouts, I know what men can feel,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve seen the tears from hard eyes slip as drops from polished steel.

آ آ آ آ I learned what college had to teach, and in the school of men
آ آ آ آ By camp-fires I have learned, or, say, unlearned it all again;
آ آ آ آ But this Iï؟½ve learned, that truth is strong, and if a man go straight
آ آ آ آ Heï؟½ll live to see his enemy struck down by time and fate!

آ آ آ آ We hold him true whoï؟½s true to one however false he be
آ آ آ آ (Thereï؟½s something wrong with every ship that lies beside the quay);
آ آ آ آ We lend and borrow, laugh and joke, and when the past is drowned,
آ آ آ آ We sit upon our swags and smoke and watch the world go round.
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
